name: Thrall
shortDescription: In Norse mythology, Thrall is one of the three classes of humanity created by the god Heimdall (as Rig) in the Eddic poem Rígsþula. He represents the class of slaves or serfs.
description: Thrall, in Norse mythology, is not a deity in the conventional sense but rather one of the three archetypal human classes created by the god Heimdall, also known as Rig, in the poem Rígsþula found within the Poetic Edda. Rig, wandering the earth in human guise, visited three different couples, each representing a different social stratum. The first couple, Ai and Edda (Great-Grandparent and Grandmother), lived a humble life and from them sprang the race of Thralls, the slaves. Thrall is described as dark-skinned, ugly, with coarse hair and a bent back, representing the physical hardships of his station. His offspring were numerous, and their names reflect the laborious tasks and unattractive qualities associated with servitude. The Rígsþula provides a social commentary on the origins of social classes in Norse society, attributing them to divine intervention. Thrall's existence highlights the importance of labor and the acceptance of social hierarchy within the Norse worldview. The poem doesn't depict Thrall as a worshipped figure, but his existence as a created being within the Norse cosmological framework suggests a divine origin for the social structure of the time.
